name: C/G
desc: Brings the fifth to the bass for a lighter, more open sound. Moving the bass note down creates smoother voice leading between progressions.

name: C/E
desc: Places the third in the bass for a brightened, more major sound. Common in pop progressions and contemporary worship music for emotional lift.

name: D/F#
desc: A classic second-inversion chord creating natural voice leading in progressions. The rising bass line feels inevitable and drives the harmony forward.

name: G/B
desc: The third in the bass creates elegant voice leading, essential in many classic pop and rock progressions. This inversion opens up the harmonic space.

name: Am/E
desc: The fifth in the bass creates a suspended, introspective quality. This voicing adds emotional depth to chord progressions in singer-songwriter and indie music.

name: Am/G
desc: A favorite in acoustic ballads, this slash chord creates a descending bass line when used in sequence. The major seventh-like sound adds sophistication.

name: F/A
desc: Puts the third over the root, creating a suspended sound without resolving. Used in film scores and classical pieces for ambiguous, searching qualities.

name: E/G#
desc: The major third in the bass emphasizes the brightness of E major. This voicing is popular in gospel and contemporary worship for its uplifting character.

name: Em/B
desc: The fifth in the bass of Em creates an open, resonant sound. Perfect for creating textural layers in alternative rock and shoegaze arrangements.

name: Dm/F
desc: A minor chord with major third in the bass creates harmonic interest. Used in rock and punk for its unstable, energetic character.

name: A/C#
desc: The major third in the bass brightens the A major chord for a lifted, resolved feeling. Essential in country and folk progressions for authenticity.

name: Bb/D
desc: Puts the third in the bass for a lifted sound that moves naturally in band arrangements. Common in soul, funk, and contemporary R&B groove patterns.
